comment_text.sn6.4:1.2: The opening is shared with MN 49, except there the Buddha recalls an encounter at Ukkaṭṭhā. See also Ja 405.
comment_text.sn6.4:1.3: Baka maintains the same wrong view at MN 49:2.2, where he engages the Buddha in philosophical debate. The word baka means “stork” or “crane”. To search for a high divinity of this name in Brahmanism is to be disappointed, for instead we find a man-eating demon (rakṣasa) in bird form whose fate is to be slain by the hero Bhīma (or Kṛṣṇa). Pali stories tell of how the stork dozes peacefully as if meditating by the water, while in reality he is trying to fool fish into approaching so he can snatch them up (Ja 38, Ja 236). A cunning, large, white, high-flying, predatory bird who fakes meditation is a fitting image for the antagonist of these suttas.
comment_text.sn6.4:1.4: The use of the impersonal pronoun (idaṁ) to refer to the self as divinity is a characteristic Upaniṣadic idiom: “you are that” (tat tvam asi, Chāndogya Upaniṣad 6.8.7); “that self is divinity” (ayam ātmā brahma, Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad 1.2); “I am that” (so’ham asmi, Īśa Upaniṣad 16); “this is that self hidden in all” (eṣa ta ātmā sarvāntaraḥ, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 3.4.1, etc.); “this, verily, is that” (etad vai tat, Kaṭha Upaniṣad 2.1.5). | For idaṁ niccaṁ see eṣa nityo (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.4.23); for idaṁ dhuvaṁ see etad apramayaṁ dhruvam (4.4.20). | “Whole” (kevala) is a Brahmanical term indicating complete union with Divinity.
comment_text.sn6.4:4.1: Neither text nor commentary is helpful here. Perhaps the number seventy-two indicates his former royal status, as Manu 7.157 tells us king’s retinue consists of six circles of twelve people. | From this point the text diverges from MN 49:5.1.
comment_text.sn6.4:5.4: For the math, see note to SN 6.10:10.1.
comment_text.sn6.4:6.1: The commentary thinks Baka is quoting the Buddha here, but I doubt it. First, the phrasing ahamasmi (“I am”) is characteristic of Brahmā (eg. DN 1:2.5.2). Second, the Buddha does not, in fact, claim to be one of “infinite vision” (anantadassī) in early Pali. Third, the text reads straightforwardly as a statement by Baka. Fourth, bhagavā is widely used as a term of respectful address in the Upaniṣads, while later Sanskrit uses anantadṛṣṭi as an epithet of Indra or Śiva. The rhetorical posture is similar to DN 11:83.6; Baka is overcompensating, his bluster betraying his insecurity.
comment_text.sn6.4:6.3: Normally it is understood that rebirth as a high Divinity requires the practice of deep meditative absorption (jhāna), which is not mentioned here. However, Baka is not asking about the kamma that led to him becoming a Divinity, but about the bodily acts he did in the past.
comment_text.sn6.4:7.1: These stories are explained at length in the commentary.
comment_text.sn6.4:7.4: This unusual phrase is, I think, suggestive of a key difference between the Upaniṣads and the Buddha. The Upaniṣads frequently treat the state of sleep and dream as a mystical union, where a person draws closer to divinity, and which appears as a mysterious analogue or manifestation of deep contemplation or realization (eg. Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.1.16 ff.). I believe the Buddha is called the “Awakened One” in deliberate contrast with that way of knowing.
comment_text.sn6.4:10.1: This shows the changeability of status. See Kesava Jātaka (Ja 346).
comment_text.sn6.4:11.1: Despite his praise, here as in MN 49 Baka does not declare himself a follower of the Buddha.

root_text.sn6.4:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn6.4:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena bakassa brahmuno evarūpaṁ pāpakaṁ diṭṭhigataṁ uppannaṁ hoti:
root_text.sn6.4:1.4: “idaṁ niccaṁ, idaṁ dhuvaṁ, idaṁ sassataṁ, idaṁ kevalaṁ, idaṁ acavanadhammaṁ, idañhi na jāyati na jīyati na mīyati na cavati na upapajjati, ito ca panaññaṁ uttari nissaraṇaṁ natthī”ti.
root_text.sn6.4:2.1: Atha kho bhagavā bakassa brahmuno cetasā cetoparivitakkamaññāya—seyyathāpi nāma balavā puriso samiñjitaṁ vā bāhaṁ pasāreyya, pasāritaṁ vā bāhaṁ samiñjeyya; evameva—jetavane antarahito tasmiṁ brahmaloke pāturahosi.
root_text.sn6.4:2.2: Addasā kho bako brahmā bhagavantaṁ dūratova āgacchantaṁ.
root_text.sn6.4:2.5: Cirassaṁ kho, mārisa, imaṁ pariyāyamakāsi yadidaṁ idhāgamanāya.
root_text.sn6.4:2.6: Idañhi, mārisa, niccaṁ, idaṁ dhuvaṁ, idaṁ sassataṁ, idaṁ kevalaṁ, idaṁ acavanadhammaṁ, idañhi na jāyati na jīyati na mīyati na cavati na upapajjati. Ito ca panaññaṁ uttari nissaraṇaṁ natthī”ti.
root_text.sn6.4:3.1: Evaṁ vutte, bhagavā bakaṁ brahmānaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn6.4:3.2: “avijjāgato vata bho bako brahmā, avijjāgato vata bho bako brahmā.
root_text.sn6.4:3.3: Yatra hi nāma aniccaṁyeva samānaṁ niccanti vakkhati, adhuvaṁyeva samānaṁ dhuvanti vakkhati, asassataṁyeva samānaṁ sassatanti vakkhati, akevalaṁyeva samānaṁ kevalanti vakkhati, cavanadhammaṁyeva samānaṁ acavanadhammanti vakkhati.
root_text.sn6.4:3.4: Yattha ca pana jāyati ca jīyati ca mīyati ca cavati ca upapajjati ca, tañca tathā vakkhati:
root_text.sn6.4:3.5: ‘idañhi na jāyati na jīyati na mīyati na cavati na upapajjati’.
root_text.sn6.4:3.6: Santañca panaññaṁ uttari nissaraṇaṁ, ‘natthaññaṁ uttari nissaraṇan’ti vakkhatī”ti.

translation_text.sn6.4:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn6.4:1.3: Now at that time Baka the Divinity had the following harmful misconception:
translation_text.sn6.4:1.4: “This is permanent, this is everlasting, this is eternal, this is whole, this is not liable to pass away. For this is where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn. And there’s no other escape beyond this.”
translation_text.sn6.4:2.1: Then the Buddha knew what Baka the Divinity was thinking. As easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, he vanished from Jeta’s Grove and reappeared in that realm of divinity.
translation_text.sn6.4:2.2: Baka the Divinity saw the Buddha coming off in the distance
translation_text.sn6.4:2.5: It’s been a long time since you took the opportunity to come here.
translation_text.sn6.4:2.6: For this is permanent, this is everlasting, this is eternal, this is complete, this is not liable to pass away. For this is where there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn. And there’s no other escape beyond this.”
translation_text.sn6.4:3.1: When he had spoken, the Buddha said to him,
translation_text.sn6.4:3.2: “Alas, Baka the Divinity is lost in ignorance! Alas, Baka the Divinity is lost in ignorance!
translation_text.sn6.4:3.3: Because what is actually impermanent, not lasting, transient, incomplete, and liable to pass away, he says is permanent, everlasting, eternal, complete, and not liable to pass away.
translation_text.sn6.4:3.4: And where there is being born, growing old, dying, passing away, and being reborn, he says that
translation_text.sn6.4:3.5: there’s no being born, growing old, dying, passing away, or being reborn.
translation_text.sn6.4:3.6: And although there is another escape beyond this, he says that there’s no other escape beyond this.”
translation_text.sn6.4:4.1: “Gotama, we seventy-two merit-makers <j>are now wielders of power,
translation_text.sn6.4:4.2: having passed beyond rebirth and old age.
translation_text.sn6.4:4.3: This is our last rebirth as the Divinity, <j>O knowledge master.
translation_text.sn6.4:4.4: And now many people pray to us.”
translation_text.sn6.4:5.1: “But, Baka, the lifespan here is short, not long,
translation_text.sn6.4:5.3: A hundred thousand times a hundred million years—
translation_text.sn6.4:6.1: “I am the blessed one of infinite vision,
translation_text.sn6.4:6.2: who has gone beyond rebirth and old age and sorrow.
translation_text.sn6.4:6.3: What was my past practice of precept and observance?
translation_text.sn6.4:6.4: Explain to me so that I may understand.”
translation_text.sn6.4:7.2: who were oppressed by thirst and heat.
translation_text.sn6.4:7.3: That’s your past practice of precept and observance.
translation_text.sn6.4:7.4: I recollect it like one who has wakened from sleep.
translation_text.sn6.4:8.1: When people at Deer River Bank were seized,
translation_text.sn6.4:8.2: you released the captives as they were led away.
translation_text.sn6.4:8.3: That’s your past practice of precept and observance.
translation_text.sn6.4:8.4: I recollect it like one who has wakened from sleep.
translation_text.sn6.4:9.1: When a boat on the Ganges River was seized
translation_text.sn6.4:9.2: by a fierce dragon desiring human flesh,
translation_text.sn6.4:9.3: you freed it wielding mighty force.
translation_text.sn6.4:9.4: That’s your past practice of precept and observance.
translation_text.sn6.4:9.5: I recollect it like one who has wakened from sleep.
translation_text.sn6.4:10.1: I used to be your footman named Kappa.
translation_text.sn6.4:10.2: You thought he was intelligent and loyal.
translation_text.sn6.4:10.3: That’s your past practice of precept and observance.
translation_text.sn6.4:10.4: I recollect it like one who has wakened from sleep.”
translation_text.sn6.4:11.1: “You certainly understand this lifespan of mine.
translation_text.sn6.4:11.2: And others, too, you know; <j>that’s why you’re the Buddha.
translation_text.sn6.4:11.3: And that’s why your blazing glory
translation_text.sn6.4:11.4: lights up even the realm of divinity.”

variant_text.sn6.4:1.4: uttari → uttariṁ (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)
